dev_plans.content: Lightweight heuristic approach using only CelebA's native attribute labels as subgroup keys. Implementation steps: (1) At end of each epoch, run the model on the full CelebA validation split in eval mode with a fixed threshold of 0.5 on the sigmoid outputs. (2) For each of the 40 attributes being predicted, partition the validation set twice: once by the 'Male' attribute (Male=+1 vs Male=-1) and once by the 'Young' attribute (Young=+1 vs Young=-1). Optionally add a third partition using 'Pale_Skin' as a crude skin-tone proxy. (3) Within each subgroup, compute true positive rate TPR = TP/(TP+FN) for the target attribute. Skip attributes where the positive count in a subgroup is below 30 to avoid noisy estimates. (4) For each attribute a and each protected axis g in {Male, Young, Pale_Skin}, compute ΔTPR_{a,g} = |TPR_{g=+1} - TPR_{g=-1}|. (5) Aggregate into a single scalar: EOD_max = max over (a, g) of ΔTPR_{a,g}. Also log the mean ΔTPR and per-attribute breakdown to a CSV file named fairness_epoch_{n}.csv. (6) Insertion point: add a hook at the end of the validation loop in train.py that calls a new function compute_fairness_metrics(preds, labels, attributes_df). (7) Artifacts produced: a CSV per epoch with columns [attribute, protected_axis, TPR_group0, TPR_group1, delta_TPR, n_group0, n_group1]; a single float EOD_max appended to a running fairness_log.jsonl; a matplotlib line plot fairness_curve.png with EOD_max over epochs and a horizontal red dashed line at the threshold. This plan requires no external fairness libraries and adds less than 200 lines of code.
dev_plans.metric: EOD_max: maximum equal-opportunity difference |TPR(g=1) - TPR(g=0)| across all 40 attributes and all protected axes (Male, Young, Pale_Skin), computed once per epoch on the CelebA validation set.
dev_plans.threshold: 0.15 — Gender Shades reported cross-subgroup gaps of 20–34% on commercial face analyzers, and vanilla ResNet-18/50 runs on CelebA routinely produce worst-case ΔTPR between 0.10 and 0.25 across the 40 attributes. Asking for <0.05 on every attribute is unrealistic for a standard (non-debiased) training run; 0.15 is a conservative ceiling that a well-tuned baseline can stay under while still flagging severely biased attributes like 'Attractive' or 'Heavy_Makeup' that typically blow past 0.2.

dev_plans.content: Statistically rigorous approach with bootstrap confidence intervals, intersectional subgroups, and multiple fairness criteria. Implementation steps: (1) Build a subgroup assignment table at dataset-load time that tags every validation image with a tuple (Male, Young, Pale_Skin), producing up to 8 intersectional cells; also allow a FairFace-labeled skin-tone column if an auxiliary CSV fairface_labels.csv is supplied. (2) At the end of each epoch, collect sigmoid logits for the entire validation set and store them in a tensor. (3) For each attribute a, compute within each subgroup s: TPR_{a,s}, FPR_{a,s}, precision, accuracy. (4) Compute three fairness criteria per attribute: Equal Opportunity Difference EOD = max_s TPR - min_s TPR; Equalized Odds Difference EOdD = max(EOD, max_s FPR - min_s FPR); Demographic Parity Difference DPD = max_s P(ŷ=1) - min_s P(ŷ=1). (5) Bootstrap: resample the validation set 500 times with replacement (stratified by subgroup), recompute each per-attribute disparity, and obtain 95% CIs. Only flag an attribute as biased if the lower CI of EOD exceeds the threshold. (6) Aggregate into a single epoch-level scalar: the trimmed mean (10% trimmed) of EOD across the 40 attributes, denoted EOD_trim_mean. This is more stable than the max and is what gets logged as the primary time-series metric. (7) Pipeline insertion: a Lightning/PyTorch validation_epoch_end hook that writes a per-epoch JSON with full per-attribute, per-subgroup statistics plus bootstrap CIs to runs/{exp}/fairness/. (8) Artifacts: per-epoch JSON; epoch-level TensorBoard scalars fairness/EOD_trim_mean, fairness/EOdD_trim_mean, fairness/DPD_trim_mean; a seaborn heatmap per epoch of attribute × subgroup TPR; a summary plot with the primary metric and its shaded bootstrap CI band plus a horizontal threshold line.
dev_plans.metric: EOD_trim_mean: 10%-trimmed mean across all 40 CelebA attributes of the per-attribute Equal Opportunity Difference max_s TPR_s − min_s TPR_s, where s ranges over the 8 intersectional subgroups defined by (Male × Young × Pale_Skin); logged once per epoch on the validation set.
dev_plans.threshold: 0.10 — the trimmed mean is much smaller than the worst-case gap, and in published CelebA baselines (e.g., Ramaswamy et al. 2021) the average per-attribute disparity for a standard CNN trained without debiasing typically sits between 0.07 and 0.12. A horizontal line at 0.10 gives a well-tuned ResNet/MobileNet run a realistic chance of staying below it for most of training while clearly flagging runs that drift toward the 0.15+ range seen in heavily biased configurations.

dev_plans.content: Tooling/library-based approach using Fairlearn and MLflow so that metrics are computed by audited code and are automatically tracked in an experiment server. Implementation steps: (1) Add dependencies: fairlearn>=0.10, mlflow>=2.9, pandas. (2) Create a FairnessCallback (Keras) or a fairness_callback.py PyTorch callback that subclasses the existing training callback interface. The callback receives at on_validation_epoch_end a dict {'y_true': ndarray[N, 40], 'y_pred': ndarray[N, 40], 'sensitive': DataFrame[N, k]} where the sensitive frame has columns ['Male', 'Young', 'Pale_Skin'] (all mapped to {0,1}). (3) For each of the 40 attributes, instantiate fairlearn.metrics.MetricFrame with metrics={'tpr': true_positive_rate, 'fpr': false_positive_rate, 'selection_rate': selection_rate, 'accuracy': accuracy_score}, passing sensitive_features=sensitive. Then call .difference(method='between_groups') to get the disparity on each metric. (4) Also call fairlearn.metrics.equalized_odds_difference and demographic_parity_difference for each attribute with the joint sensitive group (concatenated string of the three columns). (5) Aggregate into the primary scalar: worst_eod = max over attributes of equalized_odds_difference. Also compute mean_eod across attributes. (6) Log everything to MLflow: mlflow.log_metric('worst_eod', worst_eod, step=epoch); mlflow.log_metric('mean_eod', mean_eod, step=epoch); per-attribute metrics as mlflow.log_dict to an artifact fairness_{epoch}.json; generate a Fairlearn dashboard-style plot via fairlearn.metrics.MetricFrame.by_group().plot.bar(subplots=True) saved as PNG and logged as an MLflow artifact. (7) Pipeline integration: register the callback in the trainer's callback list; configure MLflow tracking URI in config.yaml; add a Grafana/Streamlit panel that reads MLflow and renders the time series with the threshold line. (8) Post-run artifact: an HTML report generated via fairlearn's reporting utilities summarizing all epochs.
dev_plans.metric: worst_eod: maximum over the 40 CelebA attributes of fairlearn.metrics.equalized_odds_difference(y_true_a, y_pred_a, sensitive_features=joint(Male, Young, Pale_Skin)), computed on the validation set at the end of every epoch and logged as an MLflow scalar.
dev_plans.threshold: 0.20 — equalized_odds_difference takes the max of the TPR gap and the FPR gap, so it is systematically larger than a pure EOD. On CelebA with a standard multi-label ResNet and joint Male×Young×Pale_Skin subgroups, the worst attribute (commonly 'Attractive', 'Heavy_Makeup', 'Wearing_Lipstick') typically shows equalized-odds gaps of 0.15–0.30 without any debiasing. A reference line at 0.20 is achievable by a well-tuned baseline yet cleanly distinguishes it from a poorly-regularized run.
